title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

Questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper investigates whether CLIP’s strong out-of-distribution (OOD) performance is primarily due to the presence of near-duplicate or highly similar images in its large pretraining dataset (LAION) versus the sheer scale of that dataset. The authors operationalize perceptual similarity using a pre-trained CLIP ViT-B/16 image embedding space. They show that certain OOD datasets (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch) have more close neighbors in LAION than in ImageNet, and use this to motivate two experiments: (1) near-pruning, which iteratively removes LAION images similar to the OOD test set and retrains; and (2) alignment of LAION to match ImageNet’s “generalization gap,” defined as the distribution of nearest-neighbor similarities from each test image to the training set. Near-pruning yields performance drops, indicating that similar training samples matter, yet the alignment experiment shows that—even after pruning to match ImageNet’s gap—models trained on LAION remain stronger than those trained solely on ImageNet. I find the overarching conclusion convincing: CLIP’s OOD strength is driven more by training data scale than by train–test similarity alone.

Strengths
- The paper is clearly written and well organized; the claims are easy to follow.
- Experiments are well defined and explicitly motivated by the central question of train–test similarity versus dataset scale.
- The methodology is careful and systematic: perceptual similarity is computed with CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings; similarity distributions are compared across training sets (LAION vs. ImageNet); near-pruning is implemented and retraining conducted; and a principled “generalization gap” matching procedure is used to test whether scale continues to confer benefits after controlling for nearest-neighbor similarity.

Weaknesses
- The empirical scope is largely confined to ImageNet and its derivatives, which may limit the generality of the conclusions.
- The absence of evaluation on unrelated OOD domains (e.g., iWildCam, FMoW) leaves open whether the findings extend beyond ImageNet-like settings.
- The similarity filtering relies solely on CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings, which may be a suboptimal proxy compared with larger CLIP variants.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Strengthen the generality claims by including experiments on OOD datasets that are not ImageNet-like (e.g., iWildCam, FMoW).
- Justify the choice of CLIP ViT-B/16 for similarity filtering or include comparisons with larger CLIP models to assess whether the conclusions are robust to the embedding choice.

Overall Assessment
This work provides a careful and informative analysis of the role of data scale versus train–test similarity in CLIP’s OOD performance. The two complementary experiments—near-pruning and generalization-gap matching—are thoughtfully designed and, together, support the conclusion that scale is a primary driver of robustness, even when controlling for nearest-neighbor similarity. The paper’s clarity and methodological rigor are notable strengths. The main limitation is the narrow empirical focus on ImageNet-like datasets and the untested assumption that ViT-B/16 embeddings suffice for similarity filtering. Addressing these points would broaden the impact and bolster the generality of the claims.

Recommendation
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper’s clarity and methodological care, along with the relevance of its conclusions, justify acceptance, though broader validation and embedding choice analysis would strengthen the contribution.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Overall assessment
This paper offers a timely and novel analysis of CLIP’s out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization by probing the role of train–test similarity. The empirical findings are compelling: even after removing nearest-neighbor overlap, CLIP maintains strong OOD performance, and a 100M LAION subset suffices to train a competitive model on OOD benchmarks. These results challenge a common hypothesis and provide valuable evidence that CLIP’s gains are not primarily explained by trivial nearest-neighbor matches. I view the contribution as meaningful and well motivated, though the evaluation scope and missing methodological details limit the strength of the conclusions. I recommend marginal acceptance.

Strengths
- Clear and original framing: examining OOD generalization through the lens of train–test distribution similarity.
- Well-designed experiments that directly test the nearest-neighbor overlap hypothesis; the negative result (overlap is not the main driver) is informative.
- Interesting practical insight that a 100M LAION subset can train a strong model for OOD tasks, underscoring the robustness of the approach beyond massive-scale data.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Evaluation scope is narrow. The study focuses on common classification OOD benchmarks but does not assess retrieval, a core CLIP application domain. This leaves an important gap in understanding how the conclusions generalize across tasks.
- Reproducibility is hindered by missing details on how nearest-neighbor sets are constructed (selection criteria, K vs. 1-NN, thresholds, sampling strategy).
- The analysis does not compare similarity distributions and local neighborhood density between the filtered LAION subsets and ImageNet-Train. Without this, it remains possible that LAION confers an advantage by providing many near-but-not-closest samples around test instances, which could influence OOD performance even when direct nearest neighbors are removed.

Requests for clarification and technical details
- Section 4.1: Please specify exactly how nearest-neighbor sets are constructed. Beyond the embedding choice, clarify:
  - The number of neighbors considered (K or 1-NN only).
  - Any sampling or filtering procedures applied to candidate neighbors.
  - The CLIP similarity score thresholds used for filtering or analysis.
- Report the exact number of neighbors/samples used in each experiment and the associated similarity thresholds to enable replication.

Additional analyses and experiments
- Retrieval evaluation: Since retrieval is central to CLIP’s use, include a retrieval study. For instance, use MSCOCO as the in-domain source for filtering and evaluate OOD retrieval on Flickr30k.
- Broader benchmarks: Add experiments on more diverse benchmarks such as VTAB (as suggested by DataComp) to better assess generality beyond standard classification OOD tests.
- Fairness and residual advantages: Provide a quantitative comparison of nearest-neighbor similarity distributions between the filtered LAION subsets (Sections 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Additionally, analyze local neighborhood density—i.e., whether LAION retains many near-but-not-closest samples around test images—so as to rule out residual advantages not captured by removing the single closest neighbor.

Broader impact and applications
- The paper would benefit from a brief discussion of how these findings might inform future data curation, filtering strategies, or training practices for CLIP-like models. Clarifying practical implications will help readers translate the insights into engineering decisions.

Conclusion
The paper presents a valuable and thoughtfully executed analysis with convincing core experiments indicating that CLIP’s OOD strength is not primarily driven by nearest-neighbor overlap. However, the limited scope and missing methodological details temper the generality of the claims. Addressing retrieval, diversifying benchmarks, and providing the requested comparisons and details would substantially strengthen the work. I recommend acceptance on the margin.

Recommendation: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The review appreciates the paper's novel analysis of CLIP's OOD generalization via train-test similarity and finds the experiments compelling, concluding that CLIP's performance is not primarily due to nearest-neighbor overlap. However, it flags limited evaluation scope, missing experimental details, and a needed comparison of neighborhood similarity/density against ImageNet-Train.
strengths: Investigates CLIP's OOD generalization from the perspective of train-test distribution similarity.
Provides intriguing findings, including that a 100M LAION subset can train a strong model on OOD benchmarks.
Experiments are well motivated and designed, showing CLIP maintains good OOD performance even without nearest-neighbor samples.
weaknesses: Evaluation is limited to common classification OOD benchmarks and omits key CLIP application domains such as retrieval.
Missing crucial experimental details about how nearest neighbor sets are constructed (e.g., selection criteria, thresholds, number of samples).

Lacks a comparison of nearest-neighbor similarity (and neighborhood density) between filtered LAION datasets and ImageNet-Train, leaving open the possibility of an unfair advantage due to more nearby-but-not-closest samples in LAION.
suggestions: Evaluate retrieval as a core CLIP task, e.g., use MSCOCO for in-domain filtering and Flickr30k as out-of-domain.
Add experiments on more diverse benchmarks (e.g., VTAB, as suggested in the DataComp paper).
Provide detailed methodology for constructing nearest-neighbor sets (how many neighbors/samples, CLIP score thresholds, and selection process).
Compare the similarity distributions and local neighborhood density around test samples between filtered LAION subsets and ImageNet-Train to rule out residual advantages.
Discuss the broader impact and potential applications of the findings for future research and engineering.
main_points: The paper offers a novel angle by probing whether CLIP's OOD strength arises from high train-test similarity and finds it is not the primary driver.
Scope is too narrow: key CLIP tasks like retrieval and broader benchmarks (e.g., VTAB) are not evaluated.
Important implementation details about nearest-neighbor construction are missing, affecting reproducibility and clarity.
A critical comparison of neighbor similarity/density between filtered LAION and ImageNet-Train is needed to validate conclusions about fairness and remaining advantages.
The reviewer seeks clarification on real-world applications and broader impact of the analysis.
technical_details: Clarify how nearest-neighbor sets in Section 4.1 are constructed (e.g., embedding choice already given, but specify K or if it's 1-NN only, sampling strategy, and any filtering procedures).
Report the number of neighbors/samples considered and the specific CLIP similarity score thresholds used for filtering or analysis.
Provide a quantitative comparison of nearest-neighbor similarity distributions between filtered LAION datasets (from Sections 5/6) and ImageNet-Train.
Analyze local neighborhood density: assess whether LAION retains many samples near the closest sample (even if not the single closest), which could confer an advantage on OOD benchmarks.

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper delivers a valuable and carefully executed empirical investigation into the sources of CLIP’s out-of-distribution (OOD) performance. The central finding—that CLIP continues to perform strongly even after reducing train–test similarity—suggests that factors beyond near-duplicate or highly similar training images contribute to its OOD gains. The experiments are comprehensive, insightful, and computationally demanding, and the release of pruned datasets and code will be useful to the community. However, the current methodology does not fully support a fair OOD claim because it prunes training samples only at the per-test-image level rather than removing the entire test domain from training. As a result, domain-relevant content likely remains in the training data, potentially inflating performance.

Strengths
- Clear empirical contribution: even with decreased train–test similarity, CLIP maintains strong test performance, indicating that OOD robustness is not solely driven by near-duplicate or highly similar examples.
- Extensive and thoughtful experimentation that will inform ongoing discussions about what drives OOD performance in large vision–language models.
- Public release of pruned datasets and code, which will facilitate replication and further analysis by the community.

Weaknesses
- The OOD evaluation is not fully fair under the current pruning strategy. Removing only training images similar to each test instance does not eliminate the broader test domain from the training set.
- Because OOD assumes an unknown test distribution, pruning based on similarity to specific test samples cannot guarantee that all relevant domain content is excluded from training.
- Residual domain content likely persists (e.g., sketches that are dissimilar to specific test sketches or sketches of non-ImageNet classes such as “space shuttle”). This implies that CLIP still “sees” the sketch domain during training and the evaluation is not truly OOD.

Main points
- The paper’s primary conclusion—that the observed OOD performance of CLIP is not primarily due to high train–test similarity—is informative and well supported by experiments.
- Nonetheless, a truly OOD-fair evaluation requires removing the entire test domain from the training data, rather than only pruning per-sample nearest neighbors.
- The presence of remaining domain-relevant images can bridge the domain gap and inflate performance, weakening strict OOD claims.
- Despite these concerns, the datasets, code, and empirical insights represent a useful contribution to the community.

Suggestions for improvement
- Perform domain-level pruning to enable a truly OOD evaluation, e.g., remove all images from the test domain (such as all “sketch”-style images) from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) and revisit ImageNet-Sketch performance under this setting.
- Add further experiments that emulate a true OOD scenario beyond per-sample similarity pruning, to more conclusively test the stated hypothesis.
- Improve clarity and concision in the abstract and the contributions list by providing a shorter, crisper summary of the key contributions.
- Rename “generalization gap” to avoid confusion with established uses of that term in the literature.

Minor comments
- Fix the typo in the sentence referencing “nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and ImageNet”; it likely should state “ImageNet-Train.”

Overall assessment
This paper makes a meaningful empirical contribution and provides resources that will help the community probe OOD behavior more rigorously. While the current evaluation does not fully establish a fair OOD setting, the work’s insights and artifacts merit recognition. I recommend acceptance at the margin.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.

Strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.

Weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.

Questions:
See weakness above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper introduces Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a hierarchical planning framework that decomposes long-horizon tasks into sub-goals generated by a sparse diffusion model (Sparse Diffuser, SD) and then solves each sub-goal segment with a standard diffusion planner at the low level. The approach improves performance, out-of-distribution generalization, and computational efficiency relative to Diffuser by shortening the high-level plan and parallelizing low-level segment planning. While the empirical gains are promising and the hierarchical formulation is appealing, the conceptual novelty feels limited and several methodological clarifications and ablations are missing. Overall, I find the work marginally above the acceptance threshold.

Strengths
- The sub-goal-based hierarchical decomposition is well-motivated and appears to enable solving more diverse and challenging scenarios than a flat diffusion planner.
- The low-level diffusion planner is positioned as task-agnostic, which is attractive for reusability across tasks once sub-goals are specified.
- SD outperforms Diffuser, which the paper convincingly connects to an increased receptive field. The reported analysis that increasing Diffuser’s kernel size also boosts performance (with weaker generalization) helps isolate receptive-field effects.
- Including dense actions in SD improves fitting the sparse objective, addressing a common bottleneck in hierarchical RL where sparse supervision can be hard to optimize.
- HD reduces computation time because the high-level sequence is shorter and low-level sub-trajectories can be planned in parallel.

Weaknesses
- Novelty feels limited: the primary technical move is to use two diffusion models—one sparse model for sub-goals and a standard model for segment plans—without a strong new algorithmic mechanism beyond this composition.
- The paper does not provide ablations or justification for using state-only diffusion (e.g., Decision-diffuser) when estimating the guidance function J, nor does it clarify whether states alone suffice in this context.
- It is unclear in Maze2D whether actions are sampled directly from the diffusion model or executed via a separate controller, as was done in Diffuser.
- Sub-goal segments are solved independently, raising concerns about suboptimality with respect to the final goal and how the method prevents segment paths from overlapping or interfering.
- The sampling strategy is unspecified: the number of trajectories sampled for high-level sub-goal sequences and for each low-level segment is not reported.
- There are feasibility concerns when actions fail to reach the exact sub-goal, especially in maze-like tasks, compounded by the absence of feedback from the low-level diffuser back to the high-level sub-goal generator.

Suggestions for improvement and clarifications
- Provide ablations with state-only diffusion (e.g., a Decision-diffuser variant) to assess whether states alone are sufficient for estimating J and to clarify what is gained by incorporating actions/features beyond states.
- Clarify the action-generation mechanism in Maze2D: are actions sampled from the diffusion model or executed via a separate controller? Please justify the design choice and its impact on performance and generalization.
- Explain how the method coordinates independent segment plans to avoid overlap and to remain consistent with the final goal. If there is any coupling or constraint between segments, detail how it is imposed.
- Report the number of sampled trajectories used for (i) generating sub-goal sequences and (ii) planning each low-level segment. This will help readers understand computational trade-offs and reproducibility.
- Describe the system’s handling of imperfect sub-goal attainment: do you use constraints, inverse dynamics, or tolerances around the sub-goal region? Given the lack of feedback from the low-level to the high-level planner, discuss practical mitigations or whether feedback could be incorporated.
- Strengthen the positioning of novelty by clearly articulating what is new relative to existing hierarchical diffusion approaches and why the combination of SD and a standard diffuser yields benefits that prior hierarchies could not.

Overall assessment
The hierarchical sub-goal formulation with diffusion models is compelling and empirically effective, with clear wins in performance, OOD generalization, and planning efficiency. The analysis around receptive field and the role of dense actions in SD is informative. However, the contribution reads as incremental, and important methodological details and ablations are missing. With additional clarifications and experiments as suggested, the paper would be significantly stronger. As it stands, I recommend a marginal accept.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.

Strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.

Weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.

Questions:
See weakness above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Overall, the contribution is viewed as marginally above the acceptance threshold.
strengths: Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models enables solving more diverse scenarios.
Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.
Sparse Diffuser (SD) outperforms Diffuser due to an increased receptive field; validated by showing larger kernel size improves performance albeit with weaker generalization.
Including dense actions in SD improves fitting of the sparse objective, addressing a common bottleneck in hierarchical RL.
HD achieves lower computation time than Diffuser because the high-level sequence is shorter and low-level segments are solved in parallel.
weaknesses: Limited novelty: the method primarily introduces two diffusers—one for sub-goal generation (sparse) and one standard diffuser for sub-goal segments.
Lack of ablations/justification regarding state-only diffusion (e.g., Decision-diffuser) and whether states alone suffice for estimating the guidance function J.
Unclear whether Maze2D uses diffusion-sampled actions or a separate controller (as in Diffuser).
Potential suboptimality from solving sub-goals independently without considering the final goal; unclear how segment paths avoid overlapping.
Unspecified sampling strategy: how many trajectories are sampled for sub-goal sequences and for individual segments.
Concerns about feasibility when actions do not reach the exact sub-goal (e.g., in maze tasks), especially given no feedback from the low-level diffuser back to the high-level diffuser.
suggestions: Provide ablations with state-only diffusion (e.g., Decision-diffuser) to assess whether states alone are sufficient for estimating J.
Clarify whether Maze2D planning uses diffusion-sampled actions or a separate controller, and justify the choice.
Explain how the method ensures segment plans do not overlap and how independent segment optimization accounts for the final goal.
Report the number of sampled trajectories for sub-goal sequences and for each segment during planning.

Describe how the system handles cases where actions fail to reach the exact sub-goal (e.g., using constraints, inverse dynamics, or tolerance), and consider adding feedback from the low-level to the high-level planner.
Strengthen the novelty positioning by clearly articulating what is new relative to existing hierarchical diffusion approaches.
main_points: HD’s hierarchical sub-goal decomposition with diffusion models improves performance, OOD generalization, and computational efficiency over Diffuser.
SD’s advantage over Diffuser is attributed to a larger receptive field; increasing Diffuser’s kernel size improves performance but weakens generalization.
Including dense actions in SD improves fitting of the sparse objective.
The contribution is viewed as incremental/limited in novelty (two diffusers for sub-goals and segments).

Methodological clarifications and ablations are needed: state-only diffusion for J, action source in Maze2D, coordination across segments and with the final goal, sampling counts, and handling failure to reach sub-goals without feedback.
technical_details: Receptive field and kernel-size: larger kernel size increases receptive field, improving performance but weakening generalization.
State-only diffusion reference: Decision-diffuser; question of whether states alone are sufficient for estimating J (the guidance/objective).
Action generation detail for Maze2D: whether diffusion-sampled actions or a separate controller is used (as in Diffuser).
Dense actions in SD help fit the sparse objective better.
Parallel low-level planning across segments reduces computation time.
Concern about exact sub-goal attainment without inverse dynamics and absence of feedback from low-level to high-level layers.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.
reviews.weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.
reviews.questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.

Strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.

Weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.

Questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a well-written and easy-to-follow hierarchical extension of Diffuser that couples a high-level sparse planner with a low-level refinement module. The approach is straightforward yet effective, and it achieves strong results on long-horizon planning and offline RL benchmarks, with particularly notable gains on AntMaze. Beyond empirical performance, the paper provides an interesting and useful analysis of how the diffusion model’s receptive field (kernel size) and jumpy steps influence both performance and data coverage.

Strengths
- Clarity and presentation: The manuscript is well-structured and clear, making the methodology and experimental setup easy to understand.
- Empirical effectiveness: The hierarchical design substantially improves outcomes on long-horizon tasks, especially on AntMaze, underscoring the value of high-level sparse planning with low-level refinement.
- Insightful analysis: The study relating kernel size (receptive field) and jumpy steps to data coverage is thoughtful and informative. The observations around coverage—e.g., that Diffuser with a small receptive field struggles during execution and can collapse to a single mode (Fig. 3)—offer concrete insights that can guide future model design.

Weaknesses
- Missing baseline: The lack of a Decision Diffuser (Ajay et al., ICLR 2023) baseline is a notable omission, particularly given the relevance of transformer-based diffusion architectures in this space.
- Architectural comparisons: The experiments are predominantly CNN-based (as in the original Diffuser), and the absence of a comparison to transformer-based approaches (e.g., Decision Diffuser) limits the completeness of the empirical evaluation.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Include Decision Diffuser (Ajay et al., 2023) as a baseline to contextualize gains relative to a transformer-based diffusion policy.
- Provide a direct comparison between the CNN-based Diffuser setup and transformer-based architectures (e.g., Decision Diffuser) to disentangle the contributions of hierarchy from those of the underlying sequence model.
- Clarify the execution-time behavior of standard Diffuser suggested by Fig. 3: does it model the full data distribution but fail to realize full coverage during actual rollouts? If so, please explain the cause of this gap and how the proposed hierarchy mitigates it.
- In Fig. 3, HD appears to cover most of the data distribution but misses two short segments/branches. Please explain why these segments are not covered and whether this is due to planner sparsity, low-level refinement limitations, or other factors.

Main Points and Assessment
- The hierarchical approach is conceptually simple and practically effective, improving long-horizon planning and offline RL performance.
- The analysis of kernel size and jumpy steps is valuable; the observation that small receptive fields hinder data coverage during execution (Fig. 3) is especially illuminating.
- Strong performance on AntMaze highlights the method’s strengths in long-horizon settings.
- However, the absence of Decision Diffuser and transformer-based comparisons weakens the broader empirical claims.

Overall Recommendation
I recommend a rating of 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper’s clarity, strong long-horizon results, and insightful analysis justify acceptance, provided the authors address the missing baseline and offer clarifications about execution-time coverage gaps and the uncovered short branches in Fig. 3.
