reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem out-of-distribution (OOD) detection when original in-distribution (ID) data suffers from class imbalance. The authors first report an experimental observation regarding two major challenges in OOD detection under the studied setting: misidentification of tail-side ID samples as OOD (False positives) and of some OOD samples as head-side ID (False negatives). They then statistically compare the learning objectives in OOD detection with class-balanced vs. -imbalanced ID data, allowing them to attribute these challenges to the auxiliary bias term that does not cancel out in the class-imbalanced scenario. Based on this analysis, they propose two unique avenues to mitigate the influence of this bias term: post-hoc normalization and train-time regularization. Together, they form a cohesive framework for OOD detection with class-imbalanced ID data, dubbed ImOOD. The authors provide substantial empirical evidence for the effectiveness of ImOOD.
reviews.strengths:
- The statistical analysis performed in the paper is sound and written in a manner that is easy to follow. The authors include a helpful intuitive understanding of the bias term that connects it back to the empirical observations about the behavior of OOD detection models under the class-imbalanced setting.
- ImOOD is well-motivated and theoretically-grounded on the the above analysis. The general thinking behind the method is sound, and for the most part, it’s described clearly enough to understand and re-implement the design.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I believe the major drawback of this paper lies in the limited scope of the studied problem. It appears that the authors assume that the class-imbalance problem only exists on the original ID dataset. I am not too convinced with the practicality of the proposed scenario; it’s hard to imagine in what deployment scenarios only the ID dataset will suffer from this problem, while the OOD dataset(s) used for training/testing is void of it. Wouldn’t it be more natural to assume that the ID training dataset can be refined in advance to make it class-balanced, while the OOD data the detector must be able to identify arise in various forms, and thus is more likely to be class-imbalanced?
    - What if the auxiliary OOD dataset used for training and/or the target OOD dataset exhibits class-imbalance as well? I think it is important to study various combinations of class-balanced and -imbalanced ID/auxiliary OOD/target OOD datasets. Does the analysis performed in the paper extend to and hold in such settings? Can ImOOD still outperform other baselines?
- Please correct me if I am wrong, but it appears that the verification of post-hoc normalization (minus the train-time regularization) on larger datasets appears to be missing. Even on CIFAR10-LT, the improvement from post-hoc normalization seems marginal, but this concern could be alleviated, as long as the improvement is consistent across various datasets. If post-hoc normalization is not quite as effective on datasets, it would signify that ImOOD is heavily reliant on the train-time regularization.
- More details on how the label distribution is learned (during test-time regularization) would be appreciated. Also, do you use the same learned label distribution when performing post-hoc normalization? Or is it used only for training-time regularization? If you discard it after training, one would have to know the label distribution anyways for post-hoc normalization, so do we really need to use the learned distribution in the first place?
- The empirical validation is limited to one target OOD dataset per InD dataset. Validation on more challenging OOD datasets (e.g., near OOD data, data with spurious correlation) would be helpful to gauge the effectiveness of the proposed method.
reviews.questions: Please refer to the Weaknesses section.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem out-of-distribution (OOD) detection when original in-distribution (ID) data suffers from class imbalance. The authors first report an experimental observation regarding two major challenges in OOD detection under the studied setting: misidentification of tail-side ID samples as OOD (False positives) and of some OOD samples as head-side ID (False negatives). They then statistically compare the learning objectives in OOD detection with class-balanced vs. -imbalanced ID data, allowing them to attribute these challenges to the auxiliary bias term that does not cancel out in the class-imbalanced scenario. Based on this analysis, they propose two unique avenues to mitigate the influence of this bias term: post-hoc normalization and train-time regularization. Together, they form a cohesive framework for OOD detection with class-imbalanced ID data, dubbed ImOOD. The authors provide substantial empirical evidence for the effectiveness of ImOOD.

Strengths:
- The statistical analysis performed in the paper is sound and written in a manner that is easy to follow. The authors include a helpful intuitive understanding of the bias term that connects it back to the empirical observations about the behavior of OOD detection models under the class-imbalanced setting.
- ImOOD is well-motivated and theoretically-grounded on the the above analysis. The general thinking behind the method is sound, and for the most part, it’s described clearly enough to understand and re-implement the design.

Weaknesses:
- I believe the major drawback of this paper lies in the limited scope of the studied problem. It appears that the authors assume that the class-imbalance problem only exists on the original ID dataset. I am not too convinced with the practicality of the proposed scenario; it’s hard to imagine in what deployment scenarios only the ID dataset will suffer from this problem, while the OOD dataset(s) used for training/testing is void of it. Wouldn’t it be more natural to assume that the ID training dataset can be refined in advance to make it class-balanced, while the OOD data the detector must be able to identify arise in various forms, and thus is more likely to be class-imbalanced?
    - What if the auxiliary OOD dataset used for training and/or the target OOD dataset exhibits class-imbalance as well? I think it is important to study various combinations of class-balanced and -imbalanced ID/auxiliary OOD/target OOD datasets. Does the analysis performed in the paper extend to and hold in such settings? Can ImOOD still outperform other baselines?
- Please correct me if I am wrong, but it appears that the verification of post-hoc normalization (minus the train-time regularization) on larger datasets appears to be missing. Even on CIFAR10-LT, the improvement from post-hoc normalization seems marginal, but this concern could be alleviated, as long as the improvement is consistent across various datasets. If post-hoc normalization is not quite as effective on datasets, it would signify that ImOOD is heavily reliant on the train-time regularization.
- More details on how the label distribution is learned (during test-time regularization) would be appreciated. Also, do you use the same learned label distribution when performing post-hoc normalization? Or is it used only for training-time regularization? If you discard it after training, one would have to know the label distribution anyways for post-hoc normalization, so do we really need to use the learned distribution in the first place?
- The empirical validation is limited to one target OOD dataset per InD dataset. Validation on more challenging OOD datasets (e.g., near OOD data, data with spurious correlation) would be helpful to gauge the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Questions:
Please refer to the Weaknesses section.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a clear statistical analysis that isolates a bias term arising under class imbalance and uses this insight to motivate the ImOOD framework. The analysis is intuitive and aligns well with observed behaviors, and the method is described with sufficient clarity to be re-implemented. Empirical results generally support the claims.

Strengths
- The statistical analysis is sound, easy to follow, and provides an intuitive explanation for the bias term under class imbalance, effectively connecting theory to empirical behavior.
- ImOOD is well-motivated by the analysis, and the framework is presented clearly enough to enable understanding and reproduction. The empirical evidence is solid within the evaluated scope.

Weaknesses
- The setting appears to assume that only the in-distribution (ID) data are class-imbalanced. This limits practical relevance, as in deployment both auxiliary OOD (used in training or calibration) and target OOD data can also be imbalanced. It is unclear whether the analysis and method extend to such cases.
- The theoretical conclusions hinge on the identified bias term, but it is not demonstrated whether these conclusions continue to hold when auxiliary and/or target OOD datasets are imbalanced.
- Post-hoc normalization yields only marginal gains on CIFAR10-LT and is not validated on larger datasets. This raises the possibility that the improvements primarily stem from the train-time regularization rather than the post-hoc component.
- Important implementation details are missing regarding how the label distribution is learned during train-time regularization and whether this learned distribution is reused for post-hoc normalization. If it is not reused, it is unclear how post-hoc normalization obtains its label distribution and why learning it during training is necessary.
- The experimental coverage is narrow: only one target OOD dataset per ID dataset is used, and more challenging OOD regimes (e.g., near-OOD, spurious correlations) are not evaluated.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Evaluate combinations where ID, auxiliary OOD, and target OOD datasets are variously balanced or imbalanced. This will test whether the analysis holds and whether ImOOD maintains its advantage under more realistic conditions.
- Report results for post-hoc normalization as a standalone component (without train-time regularization) on larger datasets to assess its consistency and independent effectiveness.
- Provide a detailed description of how the label distribution is learned during training. Clarify whether the learned distribution is used during post-hoc normalization or only for train-time regularization.
- If the learned label distribution is discarded after training, justify its role given that post-hoc normalization requires a label distribution. Discuss how the post-hoc step obtains its distribution and the implications for deployment.
- Expand the experimental evaluation to include more challenging OOD benchmarks, including near-OOD and settings with spurious correlations.

Overall Assessment
The paper offers a compelling and well-explained analysis that leads to a well-motivated method with generally supportive empirical results. However, the current scope assumptions, limited exploration of OOD imbalance, modest standalone gains from post-hoc normalization (with no large-scale validation), missing details on label distribution learning and reuse, and limited OOD benchmarks constrain the strength of the claims. With the suggested clarifications and expanded experiments, the work would be significantly strengthened. Given the quality of the analysis and the clarity of the method, I recommend acceptance at the level of 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem out-of-distribution (OOD) detection when original in-distribution (ID) data suffers from class imbalance. The authors first report an experimental observation regarding two major challenges in OOD detection under the studied setting: misidentification of tail-side ID samples as OOD (False positives) and of some OOD samples as head-side ID (False negatives). They then statistically compare the learning objectives in OOD detection with class-balanced vs. -imbalanced ID data, allowing them to attribute these challenges to the auxiliary bias term that does not cancel out in the class-imbalanced scenario. Based on this analysis, they propose two unique avenues to mitigate the influence of this bias term: post-hoc normalization and train-time regularization. Together, they form a cohesive framework for OOD detection with class-imbalanced ID data, dubbed ImOOD. The authors provide substantial empirical evidence for the effectiveness of ImOOD.

Strengths:
- The statistical analysis performed in the paper is sound and written in a manner that is easy to follow. The authors include a helpful intuitive understanding of the bias term that connects it back to the empirical observations about the behavior of OOD detection models under the class-imbalanced setting.
- ImOOD is well-motivated and theoretically-grounded on the the above analysis. The general thinking behind the method is sound, and for the most part, it’s described clearly enough to understand and re-implement the design.

Weaknesses:
- I believe the major drawback of this paper lies in the limited scope of the studied problem. It appears that the authors assume that the class-imbalance problem only exists on the original ID dataset. I am not too convinced with the practicality of the proposed scenario; it’s hard to imagine in what deployment scenarios only the ID dataset will suffer from this problem, while the OOD dataset(s) used for training/testing is void of it. Wouldn’t it be more natural to assume that the ID training dataset can be refined in advance to make it class-balanced, while the OOD data the detector must be able to identify arise in various forms, and thus is more likely to be class-imbalanced?
    - What if the auxiliary OOD dataset used for training and/or the target OOD dataset exhibits class-imbalance as well? I think it is important to study various combinations of class-balanced and -imbalanced ID/auxiliary OOD/target OOD datasets. Does the analysis performed in the paper extend to and hold in such settings? Can ImOOD still outperform other baselines?
- Please correct me if I am wrong, but it appears that the verification of post-hoc normalization (minus the train-time regularization) on larger datasets appears to be missing. Even on CIFAR10-LT, the improvement from post-hoc normalization seems marginal, but this concern could be alleviated, as long as the improvement is consistent across various datasets. If post-hoc normalization is not quite as effective on datasets, it would signify that ImOOD is heavily reliant on the train-time regularization.
- More details on how the label distribution is learned (during test-time regularization) would be appreciated. Also, do you use the same learned label distribution when performing post-hoc normalization? Or is it used only for training-time regularization? If you discard it after training, one would have to know the label distribution anyways for post-hoc normalization, so do we really need to use the learned distribution in the first place?
- The empirical validation is limited to one target OOD dataset per InD dataset. Validation on more challenging OOD datasets (e.g., near OOD data, data with spurious correlation) would be helpful to gauge the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Questions:
Please refer to the Weaknesses section.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

To explain this phenomenon, the authors introduce a generalized statistical framework, termed ImOOD, to formulate the OOD detection problem on imbalanced data distribution. Consequently, the theoretical analysis reveals that there exists a class-aware bias item between balanced and imbalanced OOD detection, which contributes to the performance gap.

Summary:
When encountering inherent imbalance of in-distribution (ID) data, the paper identified two common challenges faced by different OOD detectors: misidentifying tail class ID samples as OOD, while erroneously predicting OOD samples as head class from ID. To explain this phenomenon, the authors introduce a generalized statistical framework, termed ImOOD, to formulate the OOD detection problem on imbalanced data distribution. Consequently, the theoretical analysis reveals that there exists a class-aware bias item between balanced and imbalanced OOD detection, which contributes to the performance gap.

Strengths:
1. The paper is written well and is easy to understand.
2. The studied problem is very important.
3. The results seem to outperform state-of-the-art.

Weaknesses:
1. I am curious about the possibility of this method being extended to distance-based OOD detection methods, such as Mahalanobios distance, etc. How well does the current method compare to the state-of-the-art distance-based methods?
2. I am curious about how well the current method performs on synthesized outliers (Tao et.al. 2023).
3. Could you elaborate more on a closely related baseline, Jiang et al. 2023, and discuss the similarities and differences w.r.t your work?

see above

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper tackles the important and underexplored problem of out-of-distribution (OOD) detection when the in-distribution (ID) training data are imbalanced. It clearly identifies two characteristic failure modes under imbalance—tail-class ID samples being flagged as OOD, and OOD samples being assigned to head ID classes—and proposes ImOOD, a generalized statistical framework that attributes the performance gap between balanced and imbalanced regimes to a class-aware bias term. The method is presented clearly and is easy to follow, and the reported results appear to improve over prior work.

Strengths
- Well-written and accessible, with a clear problem formulation and motivation.
- Addresses a timely and important setting: OOD detection under imbalanced ID data.
- Empirical performance appears strong, with state-of-the-art results reported across benchmarks.

Weaknesses
- The applicability of the framework to distance-based OOD detection methods (e.g., Mahalanobis) is unclear, and the experimental comparisons do not include competitive distance-based baselines.
- There is no evaluation on synthesized outliers, as used in related work (e.g., Tao et al., 2023), which leaves robustness in that setting untested.
- The discussion of and comparison with a closely related baseline (Jiang et al., 2023) is not sufficiently elaborated. The similarities and differences in assumptions and methodology, as well as relative performance, should be clarified.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Discuss how ImOOD extends to or interfaces with distance-based OOD detectors (such as Mahalanobis distance) and include empirical comparisons against state-of-the-art distance-based methods.
- Evaluate on synthesized outliers following Tao et al. (2023) to assess robustness when the OOD distribution is controlled or constructed.
- Expand the analysis relative to Jiang et al. (2023), clarifying methodological connections, differences in the underlying assumptions (especially regarding the class-aware bias perspective), and comparative results.

Overall Assessment
The paper offers a clear and theoretically motivated perspective on imbalance-induced OOD failures via a class-aware bias term and demonstrates promising empirical gains. However, broader comparisons and analyses—particularly with distance-based detectors, synthesized outlier evaluations, and a deeper engagement with Jiang et al. (2023)—are needed to fully establish the generality and competitiveness of the approach. I recommend acceptance on the margin, contingent on addressing the above points.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary: The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a generalized statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of detecting and rejecting unknown out-of-distribution (OOD) samples in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods: misclassifying tail class in-distribution (ID) samples as OOD, and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. Then, the authors propose a general framework that can lead to improved detection performance.
reviews.strengths:
The paper introduces a generalised statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of OOD detection in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. Imbalanced data distributions widely exist in real world, and a set of fundamental works have proposed to tackle this questions to boost OOD detection. It seems that there may still exist many open questions, making the research direction in this paper promising in OOD detection. 

The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods, namely misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. This identification helps in understanding the limitations of current approaches.

The proposed method  is driven by the Bayesian analysis, demonstrating the impacts of data imbalance and suggesting a general framework that can handle the distribution shift in OOD detection. The proposed framework, as claimed by the authors, can be used to handle imbalanced issue for a set of different scoring strategies, and the evaluation results further verify the power of their method against imbalance data more or less.
reviews.weaknesses:
A direct question is that if we have used learning algorithms that can handle imbalanced data (which is often the case in reality) to train the basic model, do we need to handle imbalanced data for OOD detection thereafter. 

The authors identify two cases that make previous OOD detection methods fail, i.e., misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. It is a direct conclusion and seemingly to be important, but could the authors further connect these two cases to previous works that handle imbalanced data in OOD detection and the proposed ImOOD. For example, why previous works, such as [1], will fail to discern these two cases and why ImOOD can overcome previous drawbacks. 

g should be defined in a proper position in advance. It seems that g should be the detector built upon f.

My main concern lies in the inaccurate estimation of beta, consisting of three terms that are all biased from my view. For P(i|X), a direct failure case is that when we have a detector whose g always greater than 0 (e.g., for distance based scoring), then P(i|X) will be always greater than 0.5. Thus, all data points will be taken as ID cases, making the estimation obviously a biased case. P(y|x,i) also suffers from biased estimation, due to the well known calibration failure of deep models. Such an issue, from my view, cannot be ignored in the field of OOD detection, since it is one of the main reason why MSP score is not effective in practice. The estimation of P(y) is also biased, since the training time data distribution (ID + OOD) is different from the test situation, thus n_o used for training cannot cover the real test situation. 


Detailed discussion about the hyper-parameters setting and the choice of auxiliary OOD data and the evaluation datasets should be discussed in detailed. More methods that handle OOD detection with imbalanced data should be considered here, such as [1]. More ablation studies to test the respective power of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x) should be tested.

Summary:
The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a generalized statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of detecting and rejecting unknown out-of-distribution (OOD) samples in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods: misclassifying tail class in-distribution (ID) samples as OOD, and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. Then, the authors propose a general framework that can lead to improved detection performance.

Strengths:
The paper introduces a generalised statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of OOD detection in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. Imbalanced data distributions widely exist in real world, and a set of fundamental works have proposed to tackle this questions to boost OOD detection. It seems that there may still exist many open questions, making the research direction in this paper promising in OOD detection. 

The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods, namely misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. This identification helps in understanding the limitations of current approaches.

The proposed method  is driven by the Bayesian analysis, demonstrating the impacts of data imbalance and suggesting a general framework that can handle the distribution shift in OOD detection. The proposed framework, as claimed by the authors, can be used to handle imbalanced issue for a set of different scoring strategies, and the evaluation results further verify the power of their method against imbalance data more or less.

Weaknesses:
A direct question is that if we have used learning algorithms that can handle imbalanced data (which is often the case in reality) to train the basic model, do we need to handle imbalanced data for OOD detection thereafter. 

The authors identify two cases that make previous OOD detection methods fail, i.e., misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. It is a direct conclusion and seemingly to be important, but could the authors further connect these two cases to previous works that handle imbalanced data in OOD detection and the proposed ImOOD. For example, why previous works, such as [1], will fail to discern these two cases and why ImOOD can overcome previous drawbacks. 

g should be defined in a proper position in advance. It seems that g should be the detector built upon f.

My main concern lies in the inaccurate estimation of beta, consisting of three terms that are all biased from my view. For P(i|X), a direct failure case is that when we have a detector whose g always greater than 0 (e.g., for distance based scoring), then P(i|X) will be always greater than 0.5. Thus, all data points will be taken as ID cases, making the estimation obviously a biased case. P(y|x,i) also suffers from biased estimation, due to the well known calibration failure of deep models. Such an issue, from my view, cannot be ignored in the field of OOD detection, since it is one of the main reason why MSP score is not effective in practice. The estimation of P(y) is also biased, since the training time data distribution (ID + OOD) is different from the test situation, thus n_o used for training cannot cover the real test situation. 


Detailed discussion about the hyper-parameters setting and the choice of auxiliary OOD data and the evaluation datasets should be discussed in detailed. More methods that handle OOD detection with imbalanced data should be considered here, such as [1]. More ablation studies to test the respective power of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x) should be tested.

reviews.text:
Recommendation: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)

Summary
The paper proposes ImOOD, a generalized, Bayesian-inspired framework for OOD detection under class imbalance. The work targets two prevalent failure cases—tail-class ID samples being flagged as OOD and OOD samples being absorbed by head classes—and argues that a principled decomposition can mitigate these issues. The approach is presented as broadly applicable across OOD scoring strategies and shows empirical gains. While the direction is timely and valuable, several core methodological and clarity issues remain unresolved, and the experimental study is not yet sufficient to support strong claims. I therefore recommend a marginal reject at this stage.

Strengths
- The paper addresses an important and underexplored setting: OOD detection under imbalanced data. This is practically relevant and likely to spark follow-on work.
- ImOOD is framed as a generalized statistical approach intended to be compatible with multiple OOD scoring functions, which enhances its potential impact.
- The Bayesian analysis provides an intuitive lens on how imbalance affects OOD decisions and motivates the proposed correction.
- The framework explicitly targets two common failure modes (tail-ID misclassified as OOD; OOD collapsing to head-ID), which are well-motivated.
- Empirical results indicate that ImOOD can improve performance across different scoring strategies.

Weaknesses
- It is unclear whether specialized OOD imbalance handling is necessary if the base classifier is already trained with imbalance-aware algorithms. The current exposition does not clarify when and why ImOOD is additive beyond such training strategies, nor is this validated empirically.
- The linkage between the identified failure modes, prior work on imbalanced OOD (e.g., Detecting Out-of-distribution Data through In-distribution Class Prior), and the specific mechanisms by which ImOOD overcomes prior limitations is insufficiently developed.
- The definition and placement of the OOD detector g lack clarity. As g appears to be built upon the classifier f, this relationship should be formalized early with consistent notation to avoid confusion.
- The main technical concern is potential bias in estimating the beta term, since all three components—P(i|x), P(y|x,i), and P(y)—are argued to be biased:
  - P(i|x): If g(x) > 0 for all inputs (as can occur with certain distance-based scores), then P(i|x) > 0.5 universally, biasing decisions toward in-distribution.
  - P(y|x,i): Deep models’ calibration issues undermine reliable estimation; MSP is known to be ineffective in practice for this reason.
  - P(y): The training mixture (ID + auxiliary OOD with count n_o) may not reflect the test-time prior, making the estimate of P(y) mismatched to deployment.
- Experimental reporting is under-specified: details on hyperparameters, the choice and rationale for auxiliary OOD data, and the evaluation datasets are limited.
- The comparison set is restricted. Methods explicitly designed for imbalanced OOD (e.g., the class-prior-based approach above) should be included.
- Ablations isolating the contributions of P(i|x), P(y|x,i), and P(y) are missing, making it difficult to attribute gains to specific components of the framework.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Clarify and test whether ImOOD remains necessary when the underlying classifier is trained with imbalance-aware algorithms. Provide empirical evidence to establish the additive benefit (or failure cases) of ImOOD in that setting.
- More explicitly connect the two failure modes to prior imbalanced OOD work, explain why those methods fall short on these cases, and articulate how ImOOD addresses those gaps.
- Define the detector g earlier, clearly state its dependence on the classifier f, and maintain consistent notation throughout.
- Discuss and, where possible, mitigate bias in each component of the beta term:
  - For P(i|x), address scenarios where g(x) > 0 across the board and how the method avoids biasing toward ID.
  - For P(y|x,i), consider the impact of calibration and justify the choice of confidence proxies (e.g., MSP) in light of known limitations.
  - For P(y), justify the estimation strategy given the mismatch between training mixtures and test-time priors, including the role of n_o.
- Provide full hyperparameter settings and rationales, and justify the selection of auxiliary OOD datasets and evaluation benchmarks.
- Expand the set of baselines to include approaches tailored to imbalanced OOD (e.g., Detecting Out-of-distribution Data through In-distribution Class Prior).
- Add ablations that separately measure the influence of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x,i) to clarify which elements drive performance.

Overall Assessment
ImOOD is a promising and general framework for imbalanced OOD detection, supported by a compelling motivation and encouraging empirical results. However, the current submission leaves critical questions about estimator bias, methodological clarity (particularly the role of g relative to f), and experimental completeness unanswered. Strengthening the connection to prior work, expanding the empirical analysis (including targeted baselines and ablations), and clarifying estimation choices would significantly improve the paper. As it stands, I find the work marginally below the bar for acceptance.

The paper introduces a generalised statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of OOD detection in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. Imbalanced data distributions widely exist in real world, and a set of fundamental works have proposed to tackle this questions to boost OOD detection. It seems that there may still exist many open questions, making the research direction in this paper promising in OOD detection. 

The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods, namely misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. This identification helps in understanding the limitations of current approaches.

The proposed method  is driven by the Bayesian analysis, demonstrating the impacts of data imbalance and suggesting a general framework that can handle the distribution shift in OOD detection. The proposed framework, as claimed by the authors, can be used to handle imbalanced issue for a set of different scoring strategies, and the evaluation results further verify the power of their method against imbalance data more or less.

Weaknesses:
A direct question is that if we have used learning algorithms that can handle imbalanced data (which is often the case in reality) to train the basic model, do we need to handle imbalanced data for OOD detection thereafter. 

The authors identify two cases that make previous OOD detection methods fail, i.e., misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. It is a direct conclusion and seemingly to be important, but could the authors further connect these two cases to previous works that handle imbalanced data in OOD detection and the proposed ImOOD. For example, why previous works, such as [1], will fail to discern these two cases and why ImOOD can overcome previous drawbacks. 

g should be defined in a proper position in advance. It seems that g should be the detector built upon f.

My main concern lies in the inaccurate estimation of beta, consisting of three terms that are all biased from my view. For P(i|X), a direct failure case is that when we have a detector whose g always greater than 0 (e.g., for distance based scoring), then P(i|X) will be always greater than 0.5. Thus, all data points will be taken as ID cases, making the estimation obviously a biased case. P(y|x,i) also suffers from biased estimation, due to the well known calibration failure of deep models. Such an issue, from my view, cannot be ignored in the field of OOD detection, since it is one of the main reason why MSP score is not effective in practice. The estimation of P(y) is also biased, since the training time data distribution (ID + OOD) is different from the test situation, thus n_o used for training cannot cover the real test situation. 


Detailed discussion about the hyper-parameters setting and the choice of auxiliary OOD data and the evaluation datasets should be discussed in detailed. More methods that handle OOD detection with imbalanced data should be considered here, such as [1]. More ablation studies to test the respective power of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x) should be tested.


[1] Detecting Out-of-distribution Data through In-distribution Class Prior.

Questions:
Please see the Weaknesses.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The review acknowledges ImOOD as a promising, Bayesian-inspired framework for OOD detection under imbalanced data, highlighting its generality and empirical gains. However, it raises concerns about biased estimation of key components, clarity of definitions, relation to prior work, and insufficient experimental analysis, leading to a marginally below-threshold rating.
strengths: Introduces a generalized statistical framework (ImOOD) for OOD detection under imbalanced data distributions.
Addresses two common failure modes: misclassifying tail-class ID samples as OOD and predicting OOD samples as head-class ID.
Bayesian analysis is used to explain the impact of imbalance and justify the framework.
Claims applicability across different OOD scoring strategies with empirical results showing improvements.
Targets a practically important and underexplored problem setting (imbalanced OOD detection) with potential for further research.
weaknesses: Unclear necessity of additional OOD imbalance handling if the base classifier is already trained with imbalance-aware algorithms.
Insufficient connection between the identified failure modes, prior work on imbalanced OOD (e.g., [1]), and how ImOOD specifically overcomes prior limitations.
Ambiguity in the definition and placement of the OOD detector g; it should be defined earlier and clarified as built upon f.
Main concern about biased estimation of the beta term: all three components (P(i|x), P(y|x,i), P(y)) are argued to be biased.
Potential bias in P(i|x): if g(x) > 0 always (e.g., for distance-based scoring), then P(i|x) > 0.5 for all points, leading to a biased in-distribution estimate.
Potential bias in P(y|x,i) due to calibration failure of deep models; MSP is noted as ineffective in practice for this reason.
Potential bias in P(y) because the training distribution (ID + OOD with n_o) differs from the test scenario; n_o may not reflect the true test prior.
Lack of detailed discussion on hyperparameters, the choice of auxiliary OOD data, and evaluation datasets.
Limited comparison set; additional methods that handle imbalanced OOD (e.g., [1]) should be included.
Missing ablations isolating the contributions of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x,i).
suggestions: Clarify whether OOD imbalance handling is still required when the base classifier is trained with imbalance-aware algorithms, and empirically validate this.
More explicitly connect the two identified failure modes to prior work (e.g., [1]) and explain why those methods fail on these cases and how ImOOD resolves them.
Define the detector g earlier, clarify its relationship to the classifier f, and ensure consistent notation.
Address the potential biases in estimating beta’s components: discuss and, if possible, mitigate bias in P(i|x), P(y|x,i), and P(y).
Provide detailed hyperparameter settings and rationale, and justify the choice of auxiliary OOD data and evaluation datasets.
Include more comparison baselines that target imbalanced OOD detection (e.g., [1]).
Add ablation studies to separately evaluate the influence of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x,i) on performance.
main_points: ImOOD presents a general, Bayesian-inspired framework to handle OOD detection under imbalanced data, aiming to correct failures on tail and head classes.
The two key failure modes (tail ID misdetected as OOD; OOD predicted as head ID) are important but need clearer linkage to prior work and the proposed solution.
Core concern: the beta term’s estimation is potentially biased due to issues in P(i|x), P(y|x,i), and P(y).
Clarity issues exist regarding the definition and placement of the OOD detector g relative to f.
Experimental reporting should be expanded: include more baselines (e.g., [1]), detailed settings, and targeted ablations.
technical_details: Detector g should be defined clearly and early; it appears to be built upon the classifier f.
Potential failure case for P(i|x): if g(x) > 0 for all x (e.g., with distance-based scoring), then P(i|x) > 0.5, biasing all samples toward ID.
P(y|x,i) may be biased due to calibration failures in deep models; MSP is cited as ineffective in practice.
P(y) estimation may be biased because the training mixture (ID + OOD) and the auxiliary OOD sample count n_o do not match the test distribution.
Requests for detailed hyperparameters and explicit choices of auxiliary OOD data and evaluation datasets.
Reference baseline to include: [1] Detecting Out-of-distribution Data through In-distribution Class Prior.

title: Pure Message Passing Can Estimate Common Neighbor for Link Prediction

reviews.summary: Given that the representations of isomorphic nodes are the same, their link that aggregates the representations cannot capture some structural representation like CN, and thus it is intractable to perform the link prediction task. Based on this observation, this paper investigates the widely-used MPNN and finds that MPNN is capable of capturing joint structural features theoretically and empirically. The solution is to inject orthogonality into input vectors. Equipped with the orthogonality of the input vectors, the authors propose MPLP to estimate link information and accomplish the link prediction task. The experiments show that MPLP promotes significantly in the link prediction on both attributed/non-attributed datasets.
reviews.strengths:
+ This paper is a meaningful discussion of the previous work (Zhang et al., 2021) on whether GNNs/MPNNs can capture structural link representation. As I understand, the previous work believes that GNNs/MPNNs cannot do this supposing that isomorphic nodes have the same representation. So, this paper exploits the orthogonality in the input vectors, which is reasonable and does not totally contradict the previous work. This paper is basically valuable for supplementing the link prediction capability of GNNs/MPNNs in both theorems and experiments.
+ The authors propose a further discussion that some GNNs have the ability to perform link prediction tasks on attributed datasets due to the orthogonality of attributes. This is an interesting discussion that might contribute to the incense reason why GNNs/MPNNs can perform link prediction on attributed benchmarks. The empirical findings in Table 4 bring about the phenomenon and leave the issues that are worth discussing.
+ The results demonstrate that MPLP outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines on both attributed/non-attributed benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The assumption of Gaussian distribution in the initialization of input vectors and weight matrices is reasonable for non-attributed benchmarks but it seems not to be correspondent for attributed benchmarks.
- The research focuses on the capability of MPNN for estimating common neighbors which is a critical heuristic for link prediction. In other words, MPLP injects the CN heuristic into MPNN. So, what about the link prediction performance when we directly regard CN as the node attribute? For example, the first elements of the input vectors could be substituted with CN value.
reviews.questions:
- Why are the results of SAGE on some benchmarks (Computers/Photo) different between Table 2 and Table 4?
- I am curious about when we concatenate the attribute vector with random feats, would the results be better than those with only attributes or only random feats? Since it is hard to evaluate whether attribute vectors are more important than random feats before conducting the experiments, the concatenation might achieve the better one automatically.
- What does the author mean by `MPLP holds its own’? What proves that?
- What is the potential impact when you find MPNN can promote link prediction? In what cases or scenarios will MPLP be used and benefit the industry?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Given that the representations of isomorphic nodes are the same, their link that aggregates the representations cannot capture some structural representation like CN, and thus it is intractable to perform the link prediction task. Based on this observation, this paper investigates the widely-used MPNN and finds that MPNN is capable of capturing joint structural features theoretically and empirically. The solution is to inject orthogonality into input vectors. Equipped with the orthogonality of the input vectors, the authors propose MPLP to estimate link information and accomplish the link prediction task. The experiments show that MPLP promotes significantly in the link prediction on both attributed/non-attributed datasets.

Strengths:
+ This paper is a meaningful discussion of the previous work (Zhang et al., 2021) on whether GNNs/MPNNs can capture structural link representation. As I understand, the previous work believes that GNNs/MPNNs cannot do this supposing that isomorphic nodes have the same representation. So, this paper exploits the orthogonality in the input vectors, which is reasonable and does not totally contradict the previous work. This paper is basically valuable for supplementing the link prediction capability of GNNs/MPNNs in both theorems and experiments.
+ The authors propose a further discussion that some GNNs have the ability to perform link prediction tasks on attributed datasets due to the orthogonality of attributes. This is an interesting discussion that might contribute to the incense reason why GNNs/MPNNs can perform link prediction on attributed benchmarks. The empirical findings in Table 4 bring about the phenomenon and leave the issues that are worth discussing.
+ The results demonstrate that MPLP outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines on both attributed/non-attributed benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
- The assumption of Gaussian distribution in the initialization of input vectors and weight matrices is reasonable for non-attributed benchmarks but it seems not to be correspondent for attributed benchmarks.
- The research focuses on the capability of MPNN for estimating common neighbors which is a critical heuristic for link prediction. In other words, MPLP injects the CN heuristic into MPNN. So, what about the link prediction performance when we directly regard CN as the node attribute? For example, the first elements of the input vectors could be substituted with CN value.

Questions:
- Why are the results of SAGE on some benchmarks (Computers/Photo) different between Table 2 and Table 4?
- I am curious about when we concatenate the attribute vector with random feats, would the results be better than those with only attributes or only random feats? Since it is hard to evaluate whether attribute vectors are more important than random feats before conducting the experiments, the concatenation might achieve the better one automatically.
- What does the author mean by `MPLP holds its own’? What proves that?
- What is the potential impact when you find MPNN can promote link prediction? In what cases or scenarios will MPLP be used and benefit the industry?

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Overall assessment: Accept (rating: 8)

Summary
The paper offers a clear and meaningful advancement in our understanding of when and how MPNNs can capture structural information for link prediction. By leveraging orthogonality in input vectors, the authors reconcile the known limitations of GNNs under isomorphic node representations with the observation that structural signals can nevertheless be learned. Building on this insight, the proposed MPLP method achieves strong link prediction performance on both attributed and non-attributed datasets. The theoretical framing and empirical validation are complementary and persuasive, though several assumptions, baselines, and clarifications need to be addressed to solidify the claims.

Strengths
- The paper provides a valuable and well-argued supplement to prior work (e.g., Zhang et al., 2021), showing that MPNNs can capture joint structural features when input vectors are orthogonal, thereby avoiding conflict with earlier negative results under isomorphic representations.
- It raises a thought-provoking and empirically supported point that GNNs may perform well on attributed datasets because real-world attributes can be approximately orthogonal; the observations (e.g., in Table 4) substantiate this claim.
- MPLP demonstrates competitive or superior performance to state-of-the-art baselines across both attributed and non-attributed benchmarks, indicating the practical effectiveness of the proposed approach.

Weaknesses and points needing clarification
- The assumption that input vectors and weight matrices are initialized from Gaussian distributions is reasonable for non-attributed benchmarks, but its applicability to attributed datasets is not fully justified. It is unclear how this assumption maps onto real attribute distributions.
- The approach centers on estimating common neighbors (CN), effectively injecting the CN heuristic into MPNNs. This raises the question of how much of MPLP’s performance comes from CN itself versus the proposed modeling; a direct comparison is needed.
- There is an inconsistency in the reported SAGE results between Table 2 and Table 4 on the Computers/Photo datasets that requires explanation.
- The paper states that “MPLP holds its own,” but the specific evidence that supports this claim is not clearly delineated in the text.

Suggestions for improvement
- Clarify and justify the Gaussian initialization assumption for attributed benchmarks, or discuss alternative assumptions/settings that better reflect attributed data. Explicitly articulate when the orthogonality assumption is expected to hold in practice.
- Include a baseline where CN (or related structural heuristics) is directly encoded as node attributes (e.g., substituting selected dimensions of input vectors with CN values). This will help isolate the gains attributable to MPLP beyond the heuristic itself and improve fairness.
- Reconcile the discrepancy in SAGE results between Table 2 and Table 4 on the Computers/Photo datasets with a clear explanation (e.g., differences in preprocessing, hyperparameters, splits, or evaluation protocols).
- Evaluate a variant that concatenates attribute vectors with random features to test whether orthogonality-like effects complement real attributes and to determine if performance surpasses using only attributes or only random features.
- Make the statement “MPLP holds its own” more precise by pointing to the exact tables/metrics and summarizing the relative gains or effect sizes that substantiate the claim.
- Discuss practical impact and potential industry use cases where MPLP would be advantageous, particularly scenarios where orthogonality-like conditions arise or can be induced and where CN-style signals are known to be informative.

Conclusion
This is a strong paper with a clear conceptual contribution and strong empirical results. The link between orthogonality and the ability of MPNNs to capture joint structural features is insightful, and MPLP performs well in practice across varied benchmarks. Addressing the questions around assumptions, baseline fairness, result consistency, and practical implications would further strengthen the work. I recommend acceptance.

extracted_ideas.summary: The review is positive, highlighting that the paper shows MPNNs can capture joint structural features via orthogonal inputs and proposes MPLP, which achieves strong link prediction results on both attributed and non-attributed datasets. The reviewer appreciates the theoretical and empirical contributions but raises questions about assumptions, baselines, and clarifications.
strengths: Provides a meaningful and valuable discussion supplementing prior work (Zhang et al., 2021) by showing MPNNs can capture structural link representations when leveraging orthogonality in input vectors.
Raises an interesting point that GNNs may perform well on attributed datasets due to inherent orthogonality in attributes, supported by empirical observations (e.g., Table 4).
Demonstrates that MPLP outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on both attributed and non-attributed benchmarks.
weaknesses: Assumption of Gaussian-distributed initialization for input vectors and weight matrices seems reasonable for non-attributed benchmarks but may not correspond well to attributed benchmarks.
The approach focuses on estimating common neighbors (CN), effectively injecting the CN heuristic into MPNNs; the review questions how performance compares if CN is directly used as node attributes.
suggestions: Clarify and justify the Gaussian initialization assumption for attributed benchmarks or discuss alternative assumptions/settings relevant to attributed data.

Evaluate a baseline where CN (or related structural heuristics) is directly incorporated as node attributes (e.g., substituting some input vector elements with CN values) to assess fairness and isolate gains.
Explain the discrepancy in SAGE results between Table 2 and Table 4 (Computers/Photo).
Test concatenating attribute vectors with random features to see if performance improves over using only attributes or only random features.
Clarify the statement 'MPLP holds its own' and specify the evidence that supports this claim.
Discuss the potential practical impact and scenarios where MPLP would be used and benefit industry applications.
main_points: Orthogonality in input vectors allows MPNNs to capture joint structural features for link prediction without contradicting prior limitations under isomorphic node representations.
MPLP leverages this idea and empirically outperforms existing methods on a variety of datasets.
Concerns remain about the applicability of Gaussian initialization assumptions to attributed datasets.
The method primarily centers on CN; a fair comparison should assess directly encoding CN as features.
There are unexplained inconsistencies in reported SAGE results across tables.
Further experimental variants (e.g., concatenating attributes with random features) and clearer claims about impact and evidence are requested.
technical_details: Assumption: Input vectors and weight matrices initialized from Gaussian distributions; questioned for attributed benchmarks.
Focus on CN estimation and its role as an injected heuristic into MPNNs.
Suggestion to directly encode CN as node attributes (e.g., substituting the first elements of input vectors with CN values).
Inconsistency noted between SAGE results on Computers/Photo in Table 2 vs. Table 4.
Proposal to concatenate attribute vectors with random features to potentially improve performance.
Request to substantiate the claim 'MPLP holds its own' with explicit evidence.
Questions on practical impact and industry use cases for MPLP.

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors explore the use of pure Message-Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) for link prediction in graphs. The paper starts by exploring the known limitations of MPNNs related to their permutation invariance property for link prediction and introduces an approach called Message Passing Link Predictor (MPLP) that leverages a node "signature". This feature vector essentially consists of a one-hot quasi-orthogonal vector, which the authors claim as a solution for MPNNs and more accurate link prediction. Experimental validation on 13 different graph datasets, both attributed and non-attributed is performed in order to concretely validate the claims made in the paper.
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: Pure Message-Passing for Link Prediction is not as well-understood as it should be, so the idea of enhancing GNNs to better handle the estimation of heuristic methods is a strong avenue for generating original work. MPLP does a fine job of this through it's problem formulation, equations, and theorems. It also takes care of tying in their motivations to numerous other relevant papers. 
- Quality: The spread of datasets captures a variety of domains and the experiments are structured in a ways that directly supports the author's claims.
- Clarity: The principles for the theorems and experiments are well-founded, it respects the reader's background in understanding related GNN works and keeps the discussion about any innovation presented in the paper succinct.
- Significance: The use of quasi-orthogonal vectors as a means of enhancing the message-passing capabilities of GNNs is entirely novel as far as I know, albeit it does extend principles from SOTA models. Future research could be inspired by a high-level approach that is similar to MPLP. MPLP's ability to estimate CN and DE is also a promising and significant improvement for GNN's ability to conduct link prediction. The non-attributed benchmark test, as shown in Table 1, is an interesting inclusion that speaks to the power of MPLP to handle non-standard link prediction scenarios. The inference experiments are thorough, considering the estimation of multple labels in regard to signatue dimension, inference, and ablation studies on batch-size.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The link prediction results for attributed benchmarks, as shown in Table 2 is limited in that it does not include results from all of the standard datasets: ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi, ogbl-citation2. The OGBL datasets are included as baselines in all of the included SOTA models, the results from which would serve as a direct comparison for MPLP's performance versus any SOTA method. 
- The first concern is compounded in that it is difficult to truly tell how well MPLP improves estimation of labels given that it only considers ogbl-collab versus ELPH and not the more scalable BUDDY. 
- The experiments, as shown in Table 1 and 2 only consider results for Hits@50. This seems relevant for the subsequent evaluations of inference, dimensions, and batch sizes. But, is still limited since the current SOTA models run experiments with Hits@100, Hits@20, and MRR.
- The test for the estimation capabilities seems limited by testing just GCN and SAGE. The inner product calculation seems like it should be extended to other types of popular GNNs such as: GIN and GAT. The results from which would lend more credence to the claims made about expressiveness and the effects of implementing quasi-orthogonal vectors into link prediction models. 
- The node-label estimation of CN and DE is promising. However, it seems limited in scope since it approximates just CN or DE and does not extend further to DRNL or DE+. This may be due to concerns of tractable computation but given SEAL's explicit testing of both DRNL and DE++ as labelling tricks, this seems like an important inclusion to evaluate MPLP fully.
reviews.questions:
- What was the reasoning behind not including the remaining OGBL datasets? It seems that MPLP's scalability is an important component based on the inference, dimensions, and ablation studies but why not bring the scalability to the forefront of the paper instead of placing the ablation studies in the appendix.
- The extent that GAT could be effectively tested against MPLP seems limited given that GAT relies on attention and MPLP does not have explicitly have a mechanism to consider this. However, when considering the expressiveness of MPLP with random features and it's ability to estimate triangles, why not include a provably expressive GNN to test like GIN?
- What sort of limitations would integrating DRNL into GCN or SAGE pose? Is it concern over tractability and the fact that in certain instances DRNL is similar to DE and CN?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors explore the use of pure Message-Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) for link prediction in graphs. The paper starts by exploring the known limitations of MPNNs related to their permutation invariance property for link prediction and introduces an approach called Message Passing Link Predictor (MPLP) that leverages a node "signature". This feature vector essentially consists of a one-hot quasi-orthogonal vector, which the authors claim as a solution for MPNNs and more accurate link prediction. Experimental validation on 13 different graph datasets, both attributed and non-attributed is performed in order to concretely validate the claims made in the paper.

Strengths:
- Originality: Pure Message-Passing for Link Prediction is not as well-understood as it should be, so the idea of enhancing GNNs to better handle the estimation of heuristic methods is a strong avenue for generating original work. MPLP does a fine job of this through it's problem formulation, equations, and theorems. It also takes care of tying in their motivations to numerous other relevant papers. 
- Quality: The spread of datasets captures a variety of domains and the experiments are structured in a ways that directly supports the author's claims.
- Clarity: The principles for the theorems and experiments are well-founded, it respects the reader's background in understanding related GNN works and keeps the discussion about any innovation presented in the paper succinct.
- Significance: The use of quasi-orthogonal vectors as a means of enhancing the message-passing capabilities of GNNs is entirely novel as far as I know, albeit it does extend principles from SOTA models. Future research could be inspired by a high-level approach that is similar to MPLP. MPLP's ability to estimate CN and DE is also a promising and significant improvement for GNN's ability to conduct link prediction. The non-attributed benchmark test, as shown in Table 1, is an interesting inclusion that speaks to the power of MPLP to handle non-standard link prediction scenarios. The inference experiments are thorough, considering the estimation of multple labels in regard to signatue dimension, inference, and ablation studies on batch-size.

Weaknesses:
- The link prediction results for attributed benchmarks, as shown in Table 2 is limited in that it does not include results from all of the standard datasets: ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi, ogbl-citation2. The OGBL datasets are included as baselines in all of the included SOTA models, the results from which would serve as a direct comparison for MPLP's performance versus any SOTA method. 
- The first concern is compounded in that it is difficult to truly tell how well MPLP improves estimation of labels given that it only considers ogbl-collab versus ELPH and not the more scalable BUDDY. 
- The experiments, as shown in Table 1 and 2 only consider results for Hits@50. This seems relevant for the subsequent evaluations of inference, dimensions, and batch sizes. But, is still limited since the current SOTA models run experiments with Hits@100, Hits@20, and MRR.
- The test for the estimation capabilities seems limited by testing just GCN and SAGE. The inner product calculation seems like it should be extended to other types of popular GNNs such as: GIN and GAT. The results from which would lend more credence to the claims made about expressiveness and the effects of implementing quasi-orthogonal vectors into link prediction models. 
- The node-label estimation of CN and DE is promising. However, it seems limited in scope since it approximates just CN or DE and does not extend further to DRNL or DE+. This may be due to concerns of tractable computation but given SEAL's explicit testing of both DRNL and DE++ as labelling tricks, this seems like an important inclusion to evaluate MPLP fully.

Questions:
- What was the reasoning behind not including the remaining OGBL datasets? It seems that MPLP's scalability is an important component based on the inference, dimensions, and ablation studies but why not bring the scalability to the forefront of the paper instead of placing the ablation studies in the appendix.
- The extent that GAT could be effectively tested against MPLP seems limited given that GAT relies on attention and MPLP does not have explicitly have a mechanism to consider this. However, when considering the expressiveness of MPLP with random features and it's ability to estimate triangles, why not include a provably expressive GNN to test like GIN?
- What sort of limitations would integrating DRNL into GCN or SAGE pose? Is it concern over tractability and the fact that in certain instances DRNL is similar to DE and CN?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

The non-attributed benchmark test, as shown in Table 1, is an interesting inclusion that speaks to the power of MPLP to handle non-standard link prediction scenarios. The inference experiments are thorough, considering the estimation of multple labels in regard to signatue dimension, inference, and ablation studies on batch-size.

Weaknesses:
- The link prediction results for attributed benchmarks, as shown in Table 2 is limited in that it does not include results from all of the standard datasets: ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi, ogbl-citation2. The OGBL datasets are included as baselines in all of the included SOTA models, the results from which would serve as a direct comparison for MPLP's performance versus any SOTA method. 
- The first concern is compounded in that it is difficult to truly tell how well MPLP improves estimation of labels given that it only considers ogbl-collab versus ELPH and not the more scalable BUDDY. 
- The experiments, as shown in Table 1 and 2 only consider results for Hits@50. This seems relevant for the subsequent evaluations of inference, dimensions, and batch sizes. But, is still limited since the current SOTA models run experiments with Hits@100, Hits@20, and MRR.
- The test for the estimation capabilities seems limited by testing just GCN and SAGE. The inner product calculation seems like it should be extended to other types of popular GNNs such as: GIN and GAT. The results from which would lend more credence to the claims made about expressiveness and the effects of implementing quasi-orthogonal vectors into link prediction models. 
- The node-label estimation of CN and DE is promising. However, it seems limited in scope since it approximates just CN or DE and does not extend further to DRNL or DE+. This may be due to concerns of tractable computation but given SEAL's explicit testing of both DRNL and DE++ as labelling tricks, this seems like an important inclusion to evaluate MPLP fully.

Questions:
- What was the reasoning behind not including the remaining OGBL datasets? It seems that MPLP's scalability is an important component based on the inference, dimensions, and ablation studies but why not bring the scalability to the forefront of the paper instead of placing the ablation studies in the appendix.
- The extent that GAT could be effectively tested against MPLP seems limited given that GAT relies on attention and MPLP does not have explicitly have a mechanism to consider this. However, when considering the expressiveness of MPLP with random features and it's ability to estimate triangles, why not include a provably expressive GNN to test like GIN?
- What sort of limitations would integrating DRNL into GCN or SAGE pose? Is it concern over tractability and the fact that in certain instances DRNL is similar to DE and CN?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

My main concern is that the ablation study (as evaluated in Table 5) shows that the main working component is the shortcut removal. Also, the main results reported in Tables 1 and 2 show that the new method only brings marginal improvements. Overall, I give a weak rejection.

Weaknesses:
1. The results show that the improvements of the new method in many cases are marginal.

Questions:
It is common sense that in many link prediction datasets, the common neighbor heuristics can outperform GCNs. Therefore, I think it is very interesting and important to study the connection between the message passing and the common neighbor heuristics. It is straightforward to establish a one-hot vector to encode the neighbor node information. I like the theoretical analysis in Theorem 2. However, I do not get how you derive the CN from the product operation and use them in the GNNs. I am trying to understand Figure 3, but there is no caption explaining what colors stand for. reviews.text:
Summary
This paper studies the relationship between message passing GNNs and the common neighbor (CN) heuristic, arguing that CN can be captured via orthogonality of input vectors under pure message passing. The theoretical analysis—particularly Theorem 2—is interesting, and the empirical evaluation is broad. However, the overall improvements over baselines are often marginal, and the ablation suggests that shortcut removal is the dominant contributor to the observed gains. There are also several clarity issues around derivations, implementation details (especially for walk counting in Theorem 3), and fairness of experimental comparisons. I lean toward a weak rejection but would be open to raising my score if the authors address the points below.

Strengths
- The paper investigates an interesting and timely connection between message passing GNNs and CN heuristics.
- The theoretical treatment is useful; I especially appreciated Theorem 2 and the attempt to formalize how CN can be captured within a GNN framework.
- The experimental evaluation is comprehensive in scope.

Weaknesses
- The performance gains in Tables 1 and 2 are generally marginal; given the reported means and standard deviations, many improvements do not appear statistically significant.
- The writing and presentation need substantial improvement. In particular, key methodological steps are not clearly explained.
- The ablation (Table 5) indicates that shortcut removal is the primary effective component; other proposed components seem to contribute little, which raises concerns about novelty and impact beyond shortcut removal.

Detailed comments and requests for clarification
- Core mechanism and CN derivation: The central idea—capturing CN via orthogonality and pure message passing—is straightforward. Please clarify, step by step, how CN emerges from the product/inner product operation within your GNN formulation. The linkage from Theorem 2 to an implementable module would benefit from a precise derivation and clear notation.
- Practicality of walk counting (Theorem 3): The theorem involves counting length-l walks. How is this computed in practice without enumerating all walks? Please describe the algorithmic strategy, its time and space complexity, and any approximations used. If exact counting is required, justify the computational feasibility on your datasets; otherwise, detail how you avoid the combinatorial blow-up.
- Fairness of comparisons: To ensure a level playing field, please include comparisons where your method and baselines share the same GNN backbone (e.g., GCN or SAGE) under identical training setups. This will help disentangle architectural advantages from the contribution of your proposed components.
- Empirical significance: Given that several reported gains are small relative to variance, please strengthen the empirical evidence. This could include statistical significance tests or confidence intervals to make clear which improvements are robust.
- Ablation and component contributions: Table 5 suggests shortcut removal drives most of the gains, while other components have limited effect. Please discuss why these components underperform, whether they are necessary, and how they interact with shortcut removal. Consider refining or simplifying the method if certain parts do not add measurable value.
- Writing and presentation: The paper would benefit from a careful revision for clarity and readability. As one concrete example, Figure 3 lacks a caption explaining the color coding. Please add a clear caption specifying what each color represents and ensure all figures are self-contained and interpretable.

Recommendation
Overall rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold). The topic and theory are interesting, and the evaluation is broad, but the current empirical gains are mostly marginal, the ablation points to shortcut removal as the main driver, and key methodological details remain unclear. I would be willing to raise my score if the authors clarify the CN derivation from inner products, explain the practical computation for length-l walk counting, provide fair backbone-controlled comparisons, strengthen the statistical analysis of results, improve the writing, and justify or refine components that show limited effect.
